(手塚 治虫, born 手塚 治, *Tezuka Osamu*, (1928-11-03)3 November 1928 – 9 February 1989) was a Japanese [manga artist](/wiki/Manga_artist "Manga artist"), [cartoonist](/wiki/Cartoonist "Cartoonist") and animator. Considered to be among the greatest and most influential cartoonists of all time,[[2]](#cite_note-2) his prolific output, pioneering techniques and innovative redefinitions of genres earned him such titles as "the Father of [Manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga")" (マンガの父, *Manga no Chichi*), "the Godfather of Manga" (マンガの教父, *Manga no Kyōfu*) and "the god of Manga" (マンガの神様, *Manga no Kami-sama*). Additionally, he is often considered the Japanese equivalent to [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney"), who served as a major inspiration during Tezuka's formative years.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Though this phrase praises the quality of his early manga works for children and animations, it also blurs the significant influence of his later, more literary, [gekiga](/wiki/Gekiga "Gekiga") works.

**Osamu Tezuka** (手塚 治虫, born 手塚 治, *Tezuka Osamu*, (1928-11-03)3 November 1928 – 9 February 1989) was a Japanese [manga artist](/wiki/Manga_artist "Manga artist"), [cartoonist](/wiki/Cartoonist "Cartoonist") and animator. Considered to be among the greatest and most influential cartoonists of all time,[[2]](#cite_note-2) his prolific output, pioneering techniques and innovative redefinitions of genres earned him such titles as "the Father of [Manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga")" (マンガの父, *Manga no Chichi*), "the Godfather of Manga" (マンガの教父, *Manga no Kyōfu*) and "the god of Manga" (マンガの神様, *Manga no Kami-sama*). Additionally, he is often considered the Japanese equivalent to [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney"), who served as a major inspiration during Tezuka's formative years.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Though this phrase praises the quality of his early manga works for children and animations, it also blurs the significant influence of his later, more literary, [gekiga](/wiki/Gekiga "Gekiga") works.

Inspired by the early Chinese animated film *[Princess Iron Fan](/wiki/Princess_Iron_Fan_(1941_film) "Princess Iron Fan (1941 film)")*,[[4]](#cite_note-4) Tezuka began what was known as the manga revolution in Japan with his *[New Treasure Island](/wiki/Shin_Takarajima_(manga) "Shin Takarajima (manga)")* published in 1947. His output would spawn some of the most influential, successful and well-received manga series including the children's manga *[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy")*, *[Princess Knight](/wiki/Princess_Knight "Princess Knight")* and *[Kimba the White Lion](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion "Kimba the White Lion")*, and the adult-oriented series *[Black Jack](/wiki/Black_Jack_(manga) "Black Jack (manga)")*, *[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(manga) "Phoenix (manga)")* and *[Buddha](/wiki/Buddha_(manga) "Buddha (manga)")*, all of which won several awards.

Tezuka died of [stomach cancer](/wiki/Stomach_cancer "Stomach cancer") in 1989. His death had an immediate impact on the Japanese public and other cartoonists. A museum was constructed in [Takarazuka](/wiki/Takarazuka,_Hy%C5%8Dgo "Takarazuka, Hyōgo") dedicated to his memory and life works, and Tezuka received many posthumous awards. Several animations were in production at the time of his death along with the final chapters of *Phoenix*, which were never released.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Early life (1928–1945)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life (1928–1945)")]

Tezuka was born in [Toyonaka](/wiki/Toyonaka "Toyonaka"), Osaka. He was the eldest of three children.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatten2004145-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Otaku_Encyclopedia-6) The Tezuka family were prosperous and well-educated; his father Yutaka worked in management at Sumitomo Metals, his grandfather Taro was a lawyer and his great-grandfather Ryoan and great-great-grandfather Ryosen were doctors. His mother's family had a long military history.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Later in life, he gave his mother credit for inspiring confidence and creativity through her stories. She frequently took him to the [Takarazuka Grand Theater](/wiki/Takarazuka_Grand_Theater "Takarazuka Grand Theater"), which often headlined the [Takarazuka Revue](/wiki/Takarazuka_Revue "Takarazuka Revue"), an all-female musical theater troupe. Their romantic musicals aimed at a female audience, had a large influence of Tezuka's later works, including his costume designs. Not only that, but the performers' large, sparkling eyes also had an influence on Tezuka's art style.[[8]](#cite_note-Takarazuka_eyes-8) He said that he had a profound "spirit of nostalgia" for Takarazuka.[[9]](#cite_note-Gravett_p.77-9)

When Tezuka was young, his father showed him [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Animation_Studios "Walt Disney Animation Studios") films and he became a Disney movie buff, seeing the films multiple times in a row, most famously seeing *[Bambi](/wiki/Bambi "Bambi")* more than 80 times.[[10]](#cite_note-Atom-10) Tezuka started to draw comics around his second year of elementary school, in large part inspired by Disney animation;[[10]](#cite_note-Atom-10) he drew so much that his mother would have to erase pages in his notebook in order to keep up with his output. Tezuka was also inspired by the works by [Suihō Tagawa](/wiki/Suih%C5%8D_Tagawa "Suihō Tagawa") and [Unno Juza](/wiki/Unno_Juza "Unno Juza").[[11]](#cite_note-tezukaosamu.net-11) Later in life, he would state that the most important influence on his desire to be an animator was not Disney but the experience of watching the Chinese animation *[Princess Iron Fan](/wiki/Princess_Iron_Fan_(1941_film) "Princess Iron Fan (1941 film)")* as a child.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Around his fifth school year, he found a description of a [ground beetle](/wiki/Ground_beetle "Ground beetle"), known as "Osamushi" in Japanese, in a book on insects. Its name so resembled his own name that he adopted "Osamushi" as his pen name.[[11]](#cite_note-tezukaosamu.net-11) Tezuka continued to develop his manga skills throughout his school career. During this period he created his first adept amateur works.[[11]](#cite_note-tezukaosamu.net-11)

During high school in 1944, Tezuka was drafted to work for a factory, supporting the Japanese war effort during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"); he simultaneously continued writing manga. In 1945, Tezuka was accepted into [Osaka University](/wiki/Osaka_University "Osaka University") and began studying medicine. During this time, he also began publishing his first professional works.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Early success (1946–1951)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early success (1946–1951)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Tezuka_Osamu.JPG/250px-Tezuka_Osamu.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tezuka_Osamu.JPG)

Tezuka in the 1950s

Tezuka came to the realization that he could use manga as a means of helping to convince people to care for the world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After World War II, at age 17, he published his first professional work, *[Diary of Ma-chan](/wiki/Diary_of_Ma-chan "Diary of Ma-chan")*, which was serialized in the elementary school children's newspaper *Shokokumin Shinbun* in early 1946.

Tezuka began talks with fellow manga creator [Shichima Sakai](/w/index.php?title=Shichima_Sakai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shichima Sakai (page does not exist)")[[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E9%85%92%E4%BA%95%E4%B8%83%E9%A6%AC "ja:酒井七馬")], who pitched Tezuka a story based on [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson")'s classic adventure novel, *[Treasure Island](/wiki/Treasure_Island "Treasure Island")*. Sakai promised Tezuka a publishing spot from [Ikuei Shuppan](/w/index.php?title=Ikuei_Shuppan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ikuei Shuppan (page does not exist)") if he would work on the manga. Tezuka finished the manga, only loosely basing it on the original work.[[14]](#cite_note-14) *[Shin Takarajima](/wiki/Shin_Takarajima_(manga) "Shin Takarajima (manga)")* (*New Treasure Island*) was published and became an overnight success, which began the golden age of manga, a craze comparable to [American comic book](/wiki/American_comic_book "American comic book") [Golden Age](/wiki/Golden_Age_of_Comic_Books "Golden Age of Comic Books") at the same time.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

With the success of *New Treasure Island,* Tezuka traveled to Tokyo in search of a publisher for more of his work. [Kobunsha](/wiki/Kobunsha "Kobunsha") turned Tezuka down, but [Shinseikaku](/w/index.php?title=Shinseikaku&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shinseikaku (page does not exist)") agreed to publish *The Strange Voyage of Dr. Tiger* and [Domei Shuppansha](/w/index.php?title=Domei_Shuppansha&action=edit&redlink=1 "Domei Shuppansha (page does not exist)") agreed to publish *The Mysterious Dr. Koronko*.

While still in medical school Tezuka published his first masterpieces: a trilogy of science fiction epics called *[Lost World](/wiki/Lost_World_(manga) "Lost World (manga)")* (1948), *[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(manga) "Metropolis (manga)")* (1949), and *[Nextworld](/wiki/Nextworld "Nextworld")* (1951).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Tezuka played a central role in the influential magazine *[Manga Shōnen](/wiki/Manga_Sh%C5%8Dnen "Manga Shōnen")*, which was published between 1947 and 1955, contributing some of its most influential works and helping to define its artistic and pedagogical vision. His serial *[Kimba the White Lion](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion "Kimba the White Lion")* (1950–1954) was the magazine's most popular feature, demonstrating the potential of manga to deliver emotional, cinematic storytelling with moral depth. Tezuka also wrote the instructional column *Manga Classroom* (1952–1954), which encouraged young readers to see manga as a learnable craft, inspiring many aspiring artists. His involvement was not only instrumental in elevating the magazine's prestige but also deeply formative for his own development; *Manga Shōnen* provided him a platform to experiment with narrative form and to mentor a new generation of artists.[[16]](#cite_note-:32-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 1951, Tezuka graduated from the Osaka School of Medicine[[18]](#cite_note-history-18) and published *[Ambassador Atom](/wiki/Ambassador_Atom "Ambassador Atom")*, the first appearance of the [Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy") character. That same year Tezuka joined a group known as the Tokyo Children Manga Association, consisting of other manga artists such as [Baba Noboru](/wiki/Noboru_Baba "Noboru Baba"), Ota Jiro, Furusawa Hideo, [Eiichi Fukui](/wiki/Eiichi_Fukui "Eiichi Fukui"), Irie Shigeru and Negishi Komichi.[[18]](#cite_note-history-18)

### *Astro Boy*, national fame and early animation (1952–1960)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Astro Boy, national fame and early animation (1952–1960)")]

By 1952, *[Ambassador Atom](/wiki/Ambassador_Atom "Ambassador Atom")* had proven to be an only mild success in Japan; however, one particular character became extremely popular with young boys: a [humanoid robot](/wiki/Humanoid_robot "Humanoid robot") named Atom.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchodt20074-19) Tezuka received several letters from many young boys.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchodt200720-20) Expecting success with a series based around Atom, Tezuka's producer suggested that he be given human emotions.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchodt200721-21) One day, while working at a hospital, Tezuka was punched in the face by a frustrated American [G.I.](/wiki/G.I._(military) "G.I. (military)") This encounter gave Tezuka the idea to include the theme of Atom's interaction with aliens.[[22]](#cite_note-22) On 4 February 1952, *[Tetsuwan Atom](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy")* began serialization in *[Shonen (Magazine)](/w/index.php?title=Shonen_(Magazine)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shonen (Magazine) (page does not exist)") [[jp](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%B0%91%E5%B9%B4_(%E9%9B%91%E8%AA%8C) "jp:少年 (雑誌)")]*. The character Atom and his adventures became an instant phenomenon in Japan.

Due to the success of *Tetsuwan Atom*, in 1953, Tezuka published the [shōjo manga](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Djo_manga "Shōjo manga") *[Ribon no Kishi](/wiki/Princess_Knight "Princess Knight")* (*Princess Knight*), serialized in *[Shojo Club](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Djo_Club "Shōjo Club")* from 1953 to 1956.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 1954, Tezuka first published what he would consider his life's work, *[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(manga) "Phoenix (manga)")*, which originally appeared in *Mushi Production Commercial Firm*.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Tezuka moved house in 1954, offering his [Tokiwa-sō](/wiki/Tokiwa-s%C5%8D "Tokiwa-sō") apartment to two budding manga artists he had complemented previously, Hiroshi Fujimoto and Motoo Abiko. The duo, collectively known as [Fujiko Fujio](/wiki/Fujiko_Fujio "Fujiko Fujio"), would later form careers rivaling Tezuka's in popularity and ambitions, passing on the apartment to other manga artists as time went on.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Production career (1959–1989)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Production career (1959–1989)")]

Tezuka's first work to be adapted for animation was *[Saiyuki](/wiki/Alakazam_the_Great "Alakazam the Great")*, a retelling of the Chinese story of *[Journey to the West](/wiki/Journey_to_the_West "Journey to the West")*. Produced by [Toei Animation](/wiki/Toei_Animation "Toei Animation"), Tezuka was officially credited as the director of the film. However, later crew accounts would prove that the manga artist was difficult to motivate to do work. Most of the direction was done by [Yabushita Taiji](/w/index.php?title=Yabushita_Taiji&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yabushita Taiji (page does not exist)") instead. Tezuka was eventually given the task of storyboarding the film, so that he didn't actually have to animate anything and something in the production could get done. He did not follow Toei's deadlines, and after a year of working on the project and several weeks of threats from Toei's producers, he finally delivered his 500-page storyboard so the animators could do their job in the autumn of 1959. That said, the crew found the storyboard to be entirely unpractical, lacking pacing and a clear plot for a 90-minute film, instead something that would be better told through an open-ended weekly comic like what Tezuka had been producing. This ran counter to Toei's "climax method" that had the goal of a big finish at the end for audiences to leave the cinema remembering. The script for the film was credited to [Uekusa Keinosuke](/wiki/Keinosuke_Uekusa "Keinosuke Uekusa"). The film was released as *[Alakazam the Great](/wiki/Alakazam_the_Great "Alakazam the Great")* in 1960.

That said, many of the animators were initially shocked at the amount they had to produce in such a short amount of time—amounting to a frame a day, thinking it undoable. However, Tezuka's simplified art style made the entire animation process much more efficient.

Tezuka did not enjoy his time at Toei, and he especially did not like that he felt he had no control over "his" story or the ending.[[26]](#cite_note-26) This film is recognized as a massive turning point in animation history. It introduced the use of simplified art style and [limited animation](/wiki/Limited_animation "Limited animation") as labor and cost savers. It introduced [Tsukioka Sadao](/w/index.php?title=Tsukioka_Sadao&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tsukioka Sadao (page does not exist)"), one of Tezuka's assistants, to Toei where he would later become the director of the studio's first TV series, and it introduced Tezuka to the animators he would later poach for his own studio.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 1961, Tezuka entered the animation industry in Japan by founding the production company [Mushi Productions](/wiki/Mushi_Productions "Mushi Productions") as a rival of [Toei Animation](/wiki/Toei_Animation "Toei Animation"). His initial staff was composed of animators he had met while working on [Saiyuki](/wiki/Saiyuki_(manga) "Saiyuki (manga)") that he convinced to join by paying the animators more than double what Toei was paying them as well as paying for food. Their first film was *[Tales from a Certain Street Corner](/w/index.php?title=Tales_from_a_Certain_Street_Corner&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tales from a Certain Street Corner (page does not exist)")* (*Aru Machikado no Monogatari*), an 'anti-Disney', experimental film. Just like on *Saiyuki*, Tezuka would often fall behind his own deadlines and the staff would have to pick up the slack only for Tezuka to take credit for it later.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] *Tales from a Certain Street Corner* was shown at a single special screening and featured many "tricks" that would be later standardized as labor-saving measures in the anime industry such as repeated and reversed animation cycles of characters dancing, frames being held for a long period of time. This same screening also featured the first screening of Tezuka's *[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy_(1963_TV_series) "Astro Boy (1963 TV series)")* initial two episodes eight weeks before its original broadcast on the 5 or 6 November 1962 at the [Yamaha Hall](/w/index.php?title=Yamaha_Hall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yamaha Hall (page does not exist)").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

*[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy_(1963_TV_series) "Astro Boy (1963 TV series)")* was first broadcast on New Year's Day 1963; this series would create the first successful model for animation production in Japan and would also be the first Japanese animation dubbed into English for an American audience and also created the market for children's merchandise. This is in large part because Tezuka was able to undercut his competitors, cutting costs to 2.5 million yen per episode by using techniques that would later be adopted by the television anime industry at large such as shooting on threes, stop images, repetition, sectioning, combined use, and short shots. None of these methods were invented by Tezuka or Mushi Pro, but were instead refined there. During production, the staff also found that while the short cuts were initially obvious, the use of soundscaping helped to mitigate it.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The only reason *Astro Boy* was able to survive its inception is because Tezuka was able to sell the foreign rights to [NBC Enterprises](/wiki/NBC_Enterprises "NBC Enterprises") [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] (an important distinction from [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") itself which was the entity Tezuka believed he was selling to). The American company ordered 52 episodes, a crucial investment because Mushi Pro only had four episodes in the can and only enough resources for one episode more. In the American localization, even more over the top sound effects were used to mitigate the obviously cheap animation. The use of sound would be further utilized and exemplified in other anime to follow, leading to many of the "stock" anime sound effects modern audiences are now used to.

Selling to an American market was very restrictive, though. They were not to include any indication that the show was made in Japan, they were not to have any arc that lasted more than an episode, all street signs had to be in English, there could be no religious references, "adult" themes, or nudity. Tezuka agreed to this, claiming that it would fit better with the sci-fi setting by giving the sense of a "placelessness". However, he would soon be disappointed by the American market when a Mushi Pro representative went to discuss the next year's episode order only to find out that the Americans didn't need anymore, believing that 52 episodes were more than enough to cycle through indefinitely.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Other series were subsequently adapted to animation, including [*Jungle Emperor*](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion "Kimba the White Lion") (1965), the first Japanese animated series produced in full color.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-neuvieme-32) *Jungle Emperor* was also successfully sold to NBC Enterprises who almost made Mushi Pro clothe the wild animals featured. They were finally able to negotiate "than animals were permitted to be 'naked' in natural settings, and that the depiction of black characters was permissible, as long as they were presented as 'civilized'; evil characters could still only be white."[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In the late 60s and 70s, it was clear that the rise of Mushi Pro was a short one and it was sliding into bankruptcy. Tezuka's financial model was unsustainable and the company was deeply in debt. In two desperate attempts to earn enough money to pay investors, Tezuka turned to the adult film market and produced *[A Thousand and One Nights](/wiki/A_Thousand_and_One_Nights_(1969_film) "A Thousand and One Nights (1969 film)")* (1969) and *[Cleopatra](/wiki/Cleopatra_(1970_film) "Cleopatra (1970 film)")* (1970). Both attempts failed.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Tezuka stepped down as acting director in 1968 to found a new animation studio, [Tezuka Productions](/wiki/Tezuka_Productions "Tezuka Productions"), and continued experimenting with animation late into his life. In 1973, Mushi Productions collapsed financially; the fallout would produce several influential animation production studios, including [Sunrise](/wiki/Sunrise_(company) "Sunrise (company)").

### *Gekiga* graphic novels (1967–1989)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Gekiga graphic novels (1967–1989)")]

In 1967, in response to the magazine *[Garo](/wiki/Garo_(magazine) "Garo (magazine)")* and the *[gekiga](/wiki/Gekiga "Gekiga")* movement, Tezuka created the magazine *[COM](/wiki/COM_(manga_magazine) "COM (manga magazine)")*.[[32]](#cite_note-neuvieme-32) By doing so, he radically changed his art from a cartoony, Disney-esque [slapstick](/wiki/Slapstick "Slapstick") style towards a more realistic drawing style; at the time the themes of his books became focused on an adult audience. A common element in all these books and short stories is the very dark and immoral nature of the main characters. The stories are also filled with explicit violence, erotic scenes, and crime. Through the magazine, he also wanted to recreate the vision of the *Manga Shōnen* magazine he had contributed to in the early 1950s, making it a platform for artistic experimentation and promotion of young emerging artists.[[16]](#cite_note-:32-16)

The change of his manga from aimed at children to more 'literary' *gekiga* manga started with the *[yōkai](/wiki/Y%C5%8Dkai "Yōkai")* manga *[Dororo](/wiki/Dororo "Dororo")* in 1967. This *yōkai* manga was influenced by the success of and a response to [Shigeru Mizuki](/wiki/Shigeru_Mizuki "Shigeru Mizuki")'s *[GeGeGe no Kitarō](/wiki/GeGeGe_no_Kitar%C5%8D "GeGeGe no Kitarō")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Simultaneously, he also produced *Vampires* that, like *Dororo*, also introduced a stronger, more coherent storyline and a shift in the drawing style. After these two he began his true first *gekiga* attempt with *[Swallowing the Earth](/wiki/Swallowing_the_Earth "Swallowing the Earth")*.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Dissatisfied with the result, he soon after produced *[I.L.](/w/index.php?title=I.L._(manga)&action=edit&redlink=1 "I.L. (manga) (page does not exist)")*. His work *[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(manga) "Phoenix (manga)")* began in 1967.

Besides the well-known series *Phoenix*, *[Black Jack](/wiki/Black_Jack_(manga) "Black Jack (manga)")* and *[Buddha](/wiki/Buddha_(manga) "Buddha (manga)")*, which are drawn in this style, he also produced a vast amount of [one-shots](/wiki/One-shot_(comics) "One-shot (comics)") or shorter series, such as *[Ayako](/wiki/Ayako_(manga) "Ayako (manga)")*, *[Ode to Kirihito](/wiki/Ode_to_Kirihito "Ode to Kirihito")*, *[Alabaster](/wiki/Alabaster_(manga) "Alabaster (manga)")*, *[Apollo's Song](/wiki/Apollo%27s_Song "Apollo's Song")*, *[Barbara](/w/index.php?title=Barbara_(manga)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Barbara (manga) (page does not exist)")*, *[MW](/wiki/MW_(manga) "MW (manga)")*, *[The Book of Human Insects](/wiki/The_Book_of_Human_Insects "The Book of Human Insects")*, and a large number of short stories that were later collectively published in books such as *Under the Air*, *Clockwork Apple*, *[The Crater](/wiki/The_Crater_(manga) "The Crater (manga)")*, *Melody of Iron and Other Short Stories*, and *Record of the Glass Castle*.

Tezuka would become a bit milder in narrative tone in the 1980s with his follow-up works such as *[Message to Adolf](/wiki/Message_to_Adolf "Message to Adolf")*, *Midnight*, *[Ludwig B](/wiki/Ludwig_B "Ludwig B")* (unfinished), and *[Neo Faust](/w/index.php?title=Neo_Faust&action=edit&redlink=1 "Neo Faust (page does not exist)")*.

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Death")]

Tezuka died of [stomach cancer](/wiki/Stomach_cancer "Stomach cancer") on 9 February 1989 after he was rushed into the hospital in Tokyo.[[36]](#cite_note-Patten2004-36) His last words were: "I'm begging you, let me work!", spoken to a nurse who had tried to take away his drawing equipment.[[37]](#cite_note-hinotori-37)

Although Tezuka was agnostic, he was buried in a Buddhist cemetery in Tokyo.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In 2014, it was reported that Tezuka's daughter, [Rumiko Tezuka](/w/index.php?title=Rumiko_Tezuka&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rumiko Tezuka (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%89%8B%E5%A1%9A_%E3%82%8B%E3%81%BF%E5%AD%90 "ja:手塚 るみ子")], opened a drawer to her father's desk which had been locked since his death. In it she found a half-eaten piece of chocolate, a handwritten essay about [Katsuhiro Otomo](/wiki/Katsuhiro_Otomo "Katsuhiro Otomo") in regard to his good work on [*Akira*](/wiki/Akira_(manga) "Akira (manga)"), sketches from his various projects, and a large number of erotic sketches of [anthropomorphic](/wiki/Anthropomorphism "Anthropomorphism") animals.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Style")]

Tezuka is known for his imaginative stories and stylized Japanese adaptations of Western literature. Tezuka's "cinematic" page layouts were influenced by [Milt Gross](/wiki/Milt_Gross "Milt Gross")' early graphic novel *[He Done Her Wrong](/wiki/He_Done_Her_Wrong "He Done Her Wrong")*. He read this book as a child, and its style characterized many manga artists who followed in Tezuka's footsteps.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

A key component of Tezuka's style is his extensive use of quotations, which include his allusions to popular works and adoptions of trends. For instance, he incorporated multiple varieties of depth into one frame—mirroring a breakthrough technique in the realm of Hollywood film: [deep-focus](/wiki/Deep_focus "Deep focus") cinematography.[[41]](#cite_note-:0-41) Tezuka's *Metropolis* is an exemplar for his use of this technique,[[41]](#cite_note-:0-41) as well as for the cinematic "pans and close-ups and zooms" that created the illusion of motion in his scenes.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchodt200723-42) Nonetheless: Tezuka's dyadic visual jokes[[43]](#cite_note-:1-43)—which involve the arrival of creatures at emotionally-charged scenes[[44]](#cite_note-44)—disrupt the tension, reminding the reader of "the framework of fiction" and promoting a safe "mode of identification" with the narrative.[[43]](#cite_note-:1-43)

Tezuka's quotations of real trends mark a key aspect of his style: adaptation in response to the socio-cultural situation and interests of his audience. He involved the "kiss-scene" motif due to its rising popularity in Japanese film.[[45]](#cite_note-:2-45) Tezuka juxtaposed this with elements more customary to Japan, such as the "glorification of self-sacrifice": instead of the usual happy ending, one or more of his characters would meet their demise but specifically for the sake of others.[[45]](#cite_note-:2-45) Aside from these borrowed motifs, a signature characteristic of Tezuka's style is the [Star System](/wiki/Osamu_Tezuka%27s_Star_System "Osamu Tezuka's Star System"),[[46]](#cite_note-:3-46) which refers to his casting of characters into different roles across a body of comics. His characters were modified to appear in different works, similar to how actors modify their personality and appearance to suit different performances. Influenced by film, he created bipartite characters that were constituted by the performer (or the stock character) and the performance (or the role played by the stock character).[[46]](#cite_note-:3-46) In doing so, Tezuka created space for intertextual history, references and commentary. The Star System utilized "the crossover between celebrity, actor and character" and also enabled Tezuka to involve intertextuality.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Tezuka invented the distinctive "large eyes" style of [Japanese animation](/wiki/Anime "Anime"), drawing inspiration from the eyes of the characters of the Takarazuka Revue, as well as from Western cartoon characters such as [Betty Boop](/wiki/Betty_Boop "Betty Boop"), [Mickey Mouse](/wiki/Mickey_Mouse "Mickey Mouse"), and [Bambi](/wiki/Bambi_(character) "Bambi (character)"), and from [Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney") movies.[[8]](#cite_note-Takarazuka_eyes-8)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatten2004144-48)

### Evolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Evolution")]

While the start of Tezuka's professional career involved four-panel comics like *The Diary of Mā-chan* and *A Man from Mars*, it was the *[akahon](/wiki/Akahon "Akahon")* format of *New Treasure Island*, a comic book numbering 200 pages, that attached him to fame. The *akahon* is characterized by the use of *senkashi* paper and "bright red covers", both of which enabled easy circulation.[[49]](#cite_note-:4-49) Tezuka departed from the typical expectations of *akahon* by introducing complexity in *The Mysterious Underground Man* and morality in *Magic House* and *Vampire Devils*. Moreover, he used *romaji* (English) titles alongside the Japanese equivalents, as well as a subtle color palette and intricate composition—all of which were unlike other *akahon*.[[50]](#cite_note-:5-50) Later, Tezuka's style—which favoured *akahon*—had to be reinvented to serve the demands of serialized magazines.[[51]](#cite_note-:6-51) He created the "single charismatic hero", an example of which is Atom, to capture and keep the readership's attention through the episodic narratives.[[51]](#cite_note-:6-51)

## Works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Works")]

Tezuka's complete oeuvre includes over 700 volumes, with more than 150,000 pages.[[52]](#cite_note-Wired-52)[[53]](#cite_note-TezukaStory-53) Tezuka's creations include *[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy")* (*Mighty Atom* in Japan), *[Black Jack](/wiki/Black_Jack_(manga) "Black Jack (manga)")*, *[Princess Knight](/wiki/Princess_Knight "Princess Knight")*, *[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(manga) "Phoenix (manga)")* (*Hi no Tori* in Japan), *[Kimba the White Lion](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion "Kimba the White Lion")* (*Jungle Emperor* in Japan), *[Unico](/wiki/Unico "Unico")*, *[Message to Adolf](/wiki/Message_to_Adolf "Message to Adolf")*, *[The Amazing 3](/wiki/The_Amazing_3 "The Amazing 3"),* *[Buddha](/wiki/Buddha_(manga) "Buddha (manga)"), and [Dororo](/wiki/Dororo "Dororo")*. His "life's work" was *[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(manga) "Phoenix (manga)")*—a story of life and death that he began in the 1950s and continued until his death.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatten2004199-54)

In addition, Tezuka headed the animation production studio [Mushi Production](/wiki/Mushi_Production "Mushi Production") ("Bug Production"), which pioneered TV animation in Japan.[[55]](#cite_note-foster-55)

### Bibliography (manga)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Bibliography (manga)")]

For more complete lists, see [List of Osamu Tezuka manga](/wiki/List_of_Osamu_Tezuka_manga "List of Osamu Tezuka manga") and [List of Osamu Tezuka anime](/wiki/List_of_Osamu_Tezuka_anime "List of Osamu Tezuka anime").

A complete list of his works can be found on the Tezuka Osamu Manga Museum website.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

* ***[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy")***, 1952–68. A sequel to *Captain ATOM* (1951), with Atom renamed Astro Boy in the US.[[57]](#cite_note-Mighty_Tezuka!-57) Eventually, Astro Boy would become Tezuka's most famous creation. He created the nuclear-powered, yet peace-loving, boy robot first after being punched in the face by a drunken GI.[[57]](#cite_note-Mighty_Tezuka!-57) In 1963, *Astro Boy* made its debut as the first domestically produced animated program on Japanese television. The 30-minute weekly program (of which 193 episodes were produced) led to the first craze for anime in Japan.[[58]](#cite_note-58) In America, the TV series (which consisted of 104 episodes licensed from the Japanese run) was also a hit,[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELadd20096-60) becoming the first Japanese animation to be shown on US television, although the U.S. producers downplayed and disguised the show's Japanese origins.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELadd200921-62) Several other *Astro Boy* series have been made since, as well as a 2009 animated feature film *[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy_(film) "Astro Boy (film)")*.
* ***[Kimba the White Lion](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion "Kimba the White Lion")***, 1950–54. A [shōnen manga](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dnen_manga "Shōnen manga") series created by Tezuka which was serialized in the *Manga Shōnen* magazine. An [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") based on the manga was created, broadcast in Japan from 1965 and in North America from 1966. It was the first color animated television series created in Japan.[[63]](#cite_note-63)
* ***[Princess Knight](/wiki/Princess_Knight "Princess Knight")***, 1953–68. One of Tezuka's most famous works and widely regarded as a classic, *Princess Knight* has been very influential in the manga and anime industry. Its portrayal of [gender roles](/wiki/Gender_roles "Gender roles") is ambiguously interpreted by critics; some claim it has pro-[feminist](/wiki/Feminist "Feminist") ideals and others think it expresses [misogynist](/wiki/Misogynist "Misogynist") ideals of the 1950s–60s Japanese society. Nonetheless, it would start a tradition of androgynous heroines and establish several trends in the *shōjo* genre. In fact, it is considered to be one of the first works in this genre that was narrative-focused and that portrays a female superhero.
* ***[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(manga) "Phoenix (manga)")***, 1954–88. Tezuka's most profound and ambitious work, dealing with man's quest for immortality, ranging from the distant past to the far future. The central character is the Phoenix, the physical manifestation of the cosmos, who carries within itself the power of immortality; either granted by the Phoenix or taken from the Phoenix by drinking a small amount of its blood. Other characters appear and reappear throughout the series; usually due to their reincarnation. The work remained unfinished at the time of Tezuka's death in 1989. *Phoenix* has been filmed several times, most notably as *[Phoenix 2772](/wiki/Phoenix_2772 "Phoenix 2772")* (1980). [Baku Yumemakura](/wiki/Baku_Yumemakura "Baku Yumemakura") was influenced by *Phoenix*; Yumemakura would go on to write the script for *[Boku no Son Goku](/wiki/Alakazam_the_Great "Alakazam the Great")*.
* ***[Dororo](/wiki/Dororo "Dororo")***, 1967–68, is a manga series about a boy called Hyakkimaru who has been robbed of 48 of his body parts by 48 different devils. In order for him to retrieve a stolen part, he must eliminate the devil that stole it. Hyakkimaru meets a boy thief, Dororo, and together they travel while being constantly attacked by ghosts and monsters. In 1969, the manga series was adapted into an [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") that consisted of 26 episodes. In 2019, nearly 50 years later, the manga series was re-adapted into another [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") series with 24 episodes.[[64]](#cite_note-64)
* ***[Buddha](/wiki/Buddha_(manga) "Buddha (manga)")***, 1972–83, is Tezuka's unique interpretation of the life of [Gautama Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha"), the founder of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"). The critically acclaimed series is often referred to as a gritty portrayal of the Buddha's life. The series began in September 1972 and ended in December 1983, as one of Tezuka's last [epic](/wiki/Epic_(genre) "Epic (genre)") manga works. Nearly three decades after the manga was completed, two [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") film adaptations were released in 2011 and 2014.
* ***[Black Jack](/wiki/Black_Jack_(manga) "Black Jack (manga)")***, 1973–83. The story of [Black Jack](/wiki/Black_Jack_(character) "Black Jack (character)"), a talented surgeon who operates illegally, using radical and supernatural techniques to combat rare afflictions. *Black Jack* received the Japan Cartoonists' Association Special Award in 1975 and the Koudansha Manga Award in 1977. Three *Black Jack* TV movies were released between 2000 and 2001. In fall 2004, an anime television series was aired in Japan with 61 episodes, releasing another movie afterward. A new series, titled *Black Jack 21*, started broadcasting on 10 April 2006. In September 2008, the first volume of the manga had been published in English by Vertical Publishing and more volumes are being published to this day.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Tezuka was a descendant of [Hattori Hanzō](/wiki/Hattori_Hanz%C5%8D "Hattori Hanzō"),[[65]](#cite_note-65) a famous ninja and samurai who faithfully served [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") during the [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period") in Japan.

Tezuka's childhood nickname was *gashagasha-atama*: "messy head" (*gashagasha* is slang for messy, *atama* means head).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As a child, Tezuka's arms swelled up and he became ill. He was treated and cured by a doctor, which made him also want to be a doctor. At a crossing point, he asked his mother whether he should look into doing manga full-time or whether he should become a doctor. At the time, being a manga author was not a particularly rewarding job. The answer his mother gave was: "You should work doing the thing you like most of all." Tezuka decided to devote himself to manga creation on a full-time basis. He graduated from [Osaka University](/wiki/Osaka_University "Osaka University") and obtained his medical degree, but he would later use his medical and scientific knowledge to enrich his sci-fi manga, such as *[Black Jack](/wiki/Black_Jack_(manga) "Black Jack (manga)")*.[[53]](#cite_note-TezukaStory-53)[[66]](#cite_note-Hanabatake-66)

Tezuka enjoyed [insect collecting](/wiki/Insect_collecting "Insect collecting") and [entomology](/wiki/Entomology "Entomology") (even adding the character [虫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%99%AB "wikt:虫") 'bug' to his pen name), [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney"), and [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball")—in fact, he licensed the "grown up" version of his character [Kimba the White Lion](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion "Kimba the White Lion") as the logo for the [Seibu Lions](/wiki/Seibu_Lions "Seibu Lions") of the [Nippon Professional Baseball](/wiki/Nippon_Professional_Baseball "Nippon Professional Baseball") League.[[67]](#cite_note-Osamu_Tezuka_Biography-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) A fan of [Superman](/wiki/Superman "Superman"), Tezuka was honorary chairman of Japan's Superman Fan Club.[[69]](#cite_note-About_Osamu_Tezuka-69)

In 1959 Osamu Tezuka married Etsuko Okada at a [Takarazuka](/wiki/Takarazuka,_Hy%C5%8Dgo "Takarazuka, Hyōgo") hotel.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Tezuka met [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney") in person at the [1964 New York World's Fair](/wiki/1964_New_York_World%27s_Fair "1964 New York World's Fair"). In a 1986 entry in his personal diary, Tezuka stated that Disney wanted to hire him for a potential science fiction project.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In January 1965, Tezuka received a letter from American film director [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick"), who had watched *[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy")* and wanted to invite Tezuka to be the art director of his next movie, *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")* (which was eventually released in 1968). Although flattered by Kubrick's invitation, Tezuka's schedule did not allow him to leave his studio for a year to live in England, so he had to turn down the offer. Although he was not able to work on *2001*, he loved the film, and would play its soundtrack at maximum volume in his studio to keep him awake during long nights of work.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Tezuka's son [Makoto Tezuka](/wiki/Makoto_Tezuka "Makoto Tezuka") became a film and anime director.[[67]](#cite_note-Osamu_Tezuka_Biography-67)

## Legacy and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Osamu_Tezuka&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Legacy and influence")]

Stamps were issued in Tezuka's honor in 1997. Also, beginning in 2003, the Japanese toy company [Kaiyodo](/wiki/Kaiyodo "Kaiyodo") began manufacturing a series of figurines of Tezuka's creations, including Princess Knight, Unico, the Phoenix, Dororo, Marvelous Melmo, Ambassador Magma, and many others. To date, three series of the figurines have been released.

Tezuka's legacy has continued to be honored among manga artists and animators and companies. He guided many well-known manga artists, such as [Shotaro Ishinomori](/wiki/Shotaro_Ishinomori "Shotaro Ishinomori") and [Go Nagai](/wiki/Go_Nagai "Go Nagai"). His legacy also extends to cartoonists outside of Japan. [Will Eisner](/wiki/Will_Eisner "Will Eisner") called himself an 'ardent admirer' of Tezuka's work.[[87]](#cite_note-VERTICAL-87) [Jean Giraud](/wiki/Jean_Giraud "Jean Giraud") (Mœbius) called him 'a great artist, and a fascinating personality'.[[88]](#cite_note-88) [Craig Thompson](/wiki/Craig_Thompson "Craig Thompson") called him 'Japan's greatest cartoonist'.[[87]](#cite_note-VERTICAL-87) [Art Spiegelman](/wiki/Art_Spiegelman "Art Spiegelman") compared his place in the history of manga to that of [Siddhartha](/wiki/The_Buddha "The Buddha")'s in [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism").[[87]](#cite_note-VERTICAL-87) [Chester Brown](/wiki/Chester_Brown "Chester Brown") has praised his dramatic skills, stating that they 'make the pages fly by.'[[87]](#cite_note-VERTICAL-87) [Scott McCloud](/wiki/Scott_McCloud "Scott McCloud") has stated that he had a bookshelf of untranslated Tezuka which he 'studied like the [Torah](/wiki/Torah "Torah") for hours on end'.[[89]](#cite_note-89) [Dash Shaw](/wiki/Dash_Shaw "Dash Shaw") called him 'the absolute greatest'.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Film director [Akira Kurosawa](/wiki/Akira_Kurosawa "Akira Kurosawa") was a fan of Tezuka's comics.[[91]](#cite_note-91) He owned his collected works, and sought for him to be artistic director on an [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe") adaptation which never got off the ground.[[92]](#cite_note-92) [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick") was a fan of Tezuka's 1963 *Astro Boy* anime.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Film director [Guillermo del Toro](/wiki/Guillermo_del_Toro "Guillermo del Toro") has cited Tezuka as an influence.[[94]](#cite_note-94) Video game designers such as [Shigeru Miyamoto](/wiki/Shigeru_Miyamoto "Shigeru Miyamoto") and [Yuji Horii](/wiki/Yuji_Horii "Yuji Horii") have stated that they were influenced by Tezuka's works[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) and video games such as [Mega Man](/wiki/Mega_Man "Mega Man"), [Jumping Flash!](/wiki/Jumping_Flash! "Jumping Flash!") and Chaos Seed have cited Tezuka's work as a source of influence.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) [Hideo Kojima](/wiki/Hideo_Kojima "Hideo Kojima") mentions Tezuka's work as being one of the most impressionable parts of the [Expo '70](/wiki/Expo_%2770 "Expo '70"), which he considers to be a formative experience on himself as an adolescent youth.[[100]](#cite_note-100) [Fumito Ueda](/wiki/Fumito_Ueda "Fumito Ueda") mentions Tezuka's 'One Million Year Trip: Bandar Book' as an anime which left a strong impression on him growing up.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

In '[The Top 100 Historical Persons in Japan](/wiki/The_Top_100_Historical_Persons_in_Japan "The Top 100 Historical Persons in Japan")' poll held by [Nippon Television](/wiki/Nippon_Television "Nippon Television") in 2006, Tezuka ranked 24th, the only mangaka to make the list. In a variation of the poll, asking for the 'Greatest Geniuses in History', Tezuka ranked 11th, ahead of [Fujiko F. Fujio](/wiki/Fujiko_F._Fujio "Fujiko F. Fujio") and [Shotaro Ishinomori](/wiki/Shotaro_Ishinomori "Shotaro Ishinomori"), the only other two mangaka to make the list. He also ranked 54th on the 'Greatest Heroes of History' variation, again the only mangaka on the list.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

In the 2006 list by the [Japan Media Arts Festival](/wiki/Japan_Media_Arts_Festival "Japan Media Arts Festival") asking critics, scholars, mangaka, etc. for the 'Greatest Manga of All Time', Tezuka had three works make the top 10: *[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(manga) "Phoenix (manga)")*(1st), *[Black Jack](/wiki/Black_Jack_(manga) "Black Jack (manga)")*(5th) and *[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy")*(tied 6th). He was the only figure with more than one work in the top 10.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

Tezuka was a personal friend (and apparent artistic influence) of Brazilian comic book artist [Mauricio de Sousa](/wiki/Mauricio_de_Sousa "Mauricio de Sousa"). In 2012, Maurício published a two-issue story arc in the *[Monica Teen](/wiki/Monica_Teen "Monica Teen")* comic book featuring some of Tezuka's main characters, including [Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy"), [Black Jack](/wiki/Black_Jack_(manga_character) "Black Jack (manga character)"), Sapphire, and [Kimba](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion "Kimba the White Lion"), joining Monica and her friends in an adventure in the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest") against a smuggling organization chopping down hundreds of trees. This was the first time that [Tezuka Productions](/wiki/Tezuka_Productions "Tezuka Productions") allowed overseas artists to use Tezuka's characters.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

In October 2019, a project was announced called Tezuka 2020, which is [AI](/wiki/AI "AI")-illustrated manga in his style. At first, the illustrations were distorted and horrifying, but after studying actual human faces, the illustrations looked more like his.[[105]](#cite_note-105) After looking through thousands of AI-generated pictures, one stood out, and the illustrator [Urumu Tsunogai](/w/index.php?title=Urumu_Tsunogai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Urumu Tsunogai (page does not exist)") created the new protagonist. In 2020, an AI writer-artist made by [Kioxia](/wiki/Kioxia "Kioxia") was tasked to make a new "Tezuka" manga called *[Paidon](/w/index.php?title=Paidon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Paidon (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%81%B1%E3%81%84%E3%81%A9%E3%82%93 "ja:ぱいどん")]*, which takes place in a futuristic apocalyptic society, which was released in the magazine *[*Morning*](/wiki/Morning_(magazine) "Morning (magazine)")* on 27 February 2020. It is part of the project, which will also be drawn by physical human beings such as Shigeto Ikehara, Kenichi Kiriki, and Urumu Tsunogai. Tezuka's son held a ceremony on 26 February 2020, to introduce people to the manga. The publisher of the magazine already confirmed that a sequel is in production.[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111) The manga was published in English on June 4, 2020, under the name *Phaedo*.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Osamu_Tezuka_Museum.jpg/250px-Osamu_Tezuka_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Osamu_Tezuka_Museum.jpg)

The Osamu Tezuka Manga Museum

The city of [Takarazuka, Hyōgo](/wiki/Takarazuka,_Hy%C5%8Dgo "Takarazuka, Hyōgo"), where Tezuka grew up, opened a museum in his memory.[[6]](#cite_note-Otaku_Encyclopedia-6) The Osamu Tezuka Manga Museum (宝塚市立手塚治虫記念館; lit. "Takarazuka City Tezuka Osamu Memorial Hall") was inaugurated on 25 April 1994, and has three floors (15069.47 ft2). In the basement there is an "Animation Workshop" in which visitors can make their own animation, and a mockup of the city of Takarazuka and a replica of the table where Osamu Tezuka worked.

Outside of the building's entrance, there are imitations of the hands and feet of several characters from Tezuka (as in a true walk of fame) and on the inside, the entry hall, a replica of [Princess Knight](/wiki/Princess_Knight "Princess Knight")'s furniture. On the same floor is a permanent exhibition of manga and a room for the display of anime. The exhibition is divided into two parts: Osamu Tezuka and the city of Takarazuka and Osamu Tezuka, the author.

The second floor contains, along with several exhibitions, a manga library with five hundred works of Tezuka (some foreign editions are also present), a video library, and a lounge with decor inspired by *Kimba the White Lion*.

There is also a glass sculpture that represents the planet Earth and is based on a book written by Tezuka in his childhood called *Our Earth of Glass*.
